summary:
The Fall of Tenochtitlán (August 13, 1521 AD) was the decisive military engagement of the [[event:2319|Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire]] — an 80-day siege in which Hernán Cortés and an army of Spanish soldiers and tens of thousands of Indigenous allies besieged, systematically destroyed, and conquered the Aztec capital, ending the Aztec Empire and establishing Spanish colonial power over Mexico. After the 'Noche Triste' (June 1520), when the Spanish were expelled from Tenochtitlán with enormous casualties, Cortés spent months rebuilding alliances, gathering Indigenous forces (the Tlaxcalans, Texcocans, and dozens of other groups that hated Aztec domination), and constructing 13 brigantines to control the lake on which Tenochtitlán was built.

The siege was one of the most intense urban combats in military history. The Aztec defense under the young emperor Cuauhtémoc was fierce and determined — the Aztecs rebuilt causeways the Spaniards demolished, counterattacked repeatedly, and sacrificed Spanish captives on visible temple platforms to demoralize their enemies. But a catastrophic smallpox epidemic (spreading from Cuba, where Cortés's force had originated) had already killed perhaps a third of Tenochtitlán's population, including Emperor Cuitláhuac, and continued to devastate the defenders throughout the siege. The final assault on August 13 found Cuauhtémoc captured while trying to escape by canoe; his capture ended organized resistance. The city's destruction was near-total — what had been one of the world's great cities was reduced to rubble, and Mexico City was built directly atop the ruins. Cuauhtémoc was later tortured and executed by Cortés, and remains a national hero of Mexico.
